title: Benign Prostatic Hyperplasia -- Treatment / Management -- Medical Therapy
content: Five alpha-reductase inhibitors , such as finasteride (5 mg once daily) and dutasteride (10 mg once daily), block the intraprostatic conversion of testosterone to DHT. [89] . This causes a reduction in individual cell volume and an increase in cellular apoptosis. [90] The overall effect is a reduction in prostatic tissue volume, although it takes several months to show noticeable improvement, with six months needed for maximal effectiveness. [7] As a result of treatment, serum PSA can be reduced by 50%, with prostate volume decreasing by up to 25%.

title: 5α-Reductase Inhibitors -- Indications -- Benign Prostatic Hyperplasia
content: Both finasteride and dutasteride have proven effective for treating BPH. Finasteride reduces prostatic DHT by over 90% and serum DHT by up to 70%; these reductions were dosage-independent. [1] [4] Furthermore, finasteride significantly reduces prostatic volume, which contributes to distressing urinary symptoms. [2] [5] [6] Similarly, dutasteride reduces DHT by up to 99% in both the prostate and the serum. These reductions in prostatic DHT levels significantly decrease serum prostate-specific antigen (PSA) levels by 50% and reduce prostate volume by approximately 25% over time. [3] [7] The majority of PSA reduction occurs within the first 3 months of treatment, while prostate reduction typically takes about 6 months to achieve maximal effect. However, this may require up to a year. [1] [7]

title: Male Urinary Retention: Acute and Chronic -- Treatment / Management -- 5-Alpha reductase inhibitors
content: Finasteride or dutasteride can shrink the prostate by about 20% to 25% and decrease the incidence of acute urinary retention in men with BPH. However, they require at least 6 months for optimal effect and are most beneficial in prostates >30 g in size. Patients need to be on therapy for over 1 year to prevent future episodes of retention and reduce the need for prostate surgery. 5-α-reductase inhibitors reduce PSA levels by about 50% and help reduce prostatic bleeding. See StatPearls' companion reference, " 5 alpha-reductase inhibitors ," for more information. title: Dutasteride -- Indications -- FDA-Approved Indications
content: The FDA approved dutasteride in 2001 for the management of symptomatic benign prostatic hyperplasia to alleviate symptoms and decrease the risk of acute urinary retention and the potential need for surgery. [1] Dutasteride is approved for this indication both as a monotherapy as well as a dual therapy with the alpha-blocker tamsulosin. [1] [2] [3] As per the American Urological Association (AUA 2021) guidelines, the use of 5α-reductase inhibitors (5-ARIs) like dutasteride, either alone or in combination with α-blockers, is strongly recommended for the management of benign prostatic hyperplasia/lower urinary tract symptoms. This suggestion aims to prevent the progression of benign prostatic hyperplasia and urinary tract symptoms, decrease the risks of urinary retention, and reduce the requirement for future prostate-related surgery. [4]

title: Finasteride -- Mechanism of Action
content: Research has shown that finasteride reduces prostatic DHT levels by upwards of 90% and serum DHT levels by 70%. However, increasing the dose does not necessarily result in greater serum DHT reduction. Dutasteride, in contrast, inhibits all 3 5-alpha reductase isoenzymes, leading to a 99% reduction in serum DHT levels. In treating androgenic alopecia, finasteride does not lead to a 100% reduction in DHT; hair loss is slowed but not completely halted. Patients receiving treatment for androgenic alopecia have a reversal of hair count within 12 months. [8] In treating benign prostate hyperplasia, long-term use of finasteride has been associated with a reduction in prostatic volume, thereby relieving bothersome urinary symptoms attributed to an enlarged gland. Previously published literature has demonstrated a reduced risk of urinary retention and delayed the need for surgical intervention. Upon discontinuation of finasteride, DHT levels return to normal within 14 days.

title: Tadalafil -- Administration -- Adult Dosage
content: The recommended adult doses differ based on the condition being treated. For erectile dysfunction in the adult population, tadalafil can be administered as needed at an initial dose of 10 mg at least 30 minutes before sexual activity, or it can be administered daily at a dose of 2.5 to 5mg, irrespective of sexual activity. Depending on tolerability and efficacy, dosing can be adjusted to either 5 mg or 20 mg. [19] In benign prostatic hyperplasia (BPH), the advised dosage is 5 mg once daily. [20] When using combination finasteride and tadalafil, a fixed-dose combination of finasteride 5 mg and tadalafil 5 mg is administered orally once daily without food for up to 26 weeks for benign prostatic hyperplasia. [21] The usual tadalafil dosage for pulmonary arterial hypertension (PAH) is 40 mg daily. However, this can be initiated at 20 mg daily and increased after 4 weeks. [22]

title: Dutasteride -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
content: Benign prostatic hyperplasia (BPH) is a condition linked with lower urinary tract symptoms (LUTS), and it is becoming more prevalent among the aging population. Over the last 20 years, the morbidity related to BPH and BPH-associated LUTS has surged by a substantial 80%, underscoring the escalating significance of this condition. [46] UTS not only significantly impacts patient quality of life but also exhibits more pronounced effects on depression and anxiety compared to other chronic illnesses such as gout, diabetes, and hypertension. [47] [48] [49] Effectively managing BPH, especially when accompanied by LUTS, is crucial to address these challenges.

title: UMLS -- Run 21 -- Level 2 -- Community 370
content: DRUGS USED IN BENIGN PROSTATIC HYPERTROPHY are classified as a type of UROLOGICALS, placing them within the therapeutic domain that addresses disorders of the urinary tract and male reproductive system. UROLOGICALS themselves are a subset of the broader category GENITO URINARY SYSTEM AND SEX HORMONES, linking these medications to the overall physiology of the genitourinary tract and associated hormonal regulation. Within the DRUGS USED IN BENIGN PROSTATIC HYPERTROPHY category, two more specific subclasses are recognized: Other drugs used in benign prostatic hypertrophy in ATC and Testosterone‑5‑alpha reductase inhibitors for benign prostatic hypertrophy. Both of these subclasses are further nested under the general class drugs used in benign prostatic hypertrophy, indicating that they share the common therapeutic purpose of treating benign prostatic hypertrophy. The subclass Testosterone‑5‑alpha reductase inhibitors for benign prostatic hypertrophy includes individual agents such as dutasterid, which is a member of this inhibitor class and exemplifies the pharmacologic approach of blocking the conversion of testosterone to dihydrotestosterone to reduce prostate size. This hierarchical organization clarifies how BPH medications relate to each other, to the urological therapeutic area, and to the larger genitourinary‑hormonal system, supporting coherent prescribing and patient‑care strategies.

title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 3 -- Community 1780
content: Drugs used in benign prostatic hyperplasia are classified as urologicals, a therapeutic category of medications that target disorders of the urinary tract and male reproductive system. As urologicals, these agents are employed to relieve urinary symptoms caused by an enlarged prostate, such as urinary retention, frequency, nocturia, and weak stream, by mechanisms that may include smooth‑muscle relaxation, reduction of prostate volume, or improvement of bladder outflow. Their classification as urologicals underscores their role within the broader pharmacologic management of benign prostatic hyperplasia and related urological conditions.

title: UMLS -- Run 32 -- Level 2 -- Community 1140
content: The therapeutic agents used for benign prostatic hypertrophy (BPH) are classified within the broader category of urologicals, which in turn are a sub‑domain of the genito‑urinary system and sex hormones. Within the urological drug group, the class “drugs used in benign prostatic hypertrophy” encompasses two major pharmacologic families. Alpha‑adrenoreceptor antagonists for BPH represent one family; silodosin is an example of a member of this class and functions as an alpha‑adrenoreceptor antagonist that relaxes prostatic smooth muscle to improve urinary flow. The second family consists of testosterone‑5‑alpha reductase inhibitors for BPH. Finasteride and dutasteride are individual agents that belong to this subclass; they inhibit the conversion of testosterone to dihydrotestosterone, thereby reducing prostatic volume. In addition to their role in managing BPH, finasteride may also be used to treat prostate tumors (ورم البروستاتا). Overall, the hierarchy places specific BPH drugs (alpha‑adrenoreceptor antagonists such as silodosin and 5‑alpha reductase inhibitors such as finasteride and dutasteride) under the umbrella of urological medications, which are themselves part of the larger genito‑urinary and sex‑hormone system. Understanding these relationships aids clinicians in selecting appropriate pharmacotherapy for prostate enlargement and related prostatic conditions.

content: Alpha‑adrenoreceptor antagonists that are used to treat benign prostatic hypertrophy include silodosin, terazosin, and the fixed‑dose combination dutasteride/tamsulosin. The dutasteride/tamsulosin combination is marketed as an oral capsule that contains 0.5 mg dutasteride and 0.4 mg tamsulosin hydrochloride; tamsulosin hydrochloride is one of the active ingredients of this capsule. Understanding that silodosin, terazosin, and dutasteride/tamsulosin belong to the same drug class, recognizing terazosin’s vasodilatory actions, and knowing the exact composition of the dutasteride/tamsulosin capsule helps clinicians select and manage appropriate pharmacologic therapy for patients with benign prostatic hypertrophy.

title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 1068
content: Alpha‑adrenoreceptor antagonists are a pharmacologic class employed in the treatment of benign prostatic hypertrophy (BPH). Members of this class include silodosin, a selective α1‑adrenergic blocker, and the fixed‑dose combination product dutasteride 0.5 mg/tamsulosin hydrochloride 0.4 mg oral capsule. The dutasteride/tamsulosin capsule contains two active ingredients—dutasteride, a 5‑α‑reductase inhibitor, and tamsulosin hydrochloride, an α1‑adrenergic antagonist—both of which contribute to prostate size reduction and smooth‑muscle relaxation in BPH. Peripherally acting α‑adrenoreceptor antagonists are also part of this therapeutic group. All agents within the α‑adrenoreceptor antagonist class, including silodosin, dutasteride/tamsulosin, and peripherally acting variants, share a common safety profile: they may be associated with poisoning, adverse effects, and risks related to underdosing. Recognizing these interrelationships is essential for optimizing BPH management while minimizing potential toxicities.

content: Doxazosin and its mesylate salt, doxazosin‑mesilát, are recognized as therapeutic agents for hyperplasia of the prostate. Other α₁‑adrenergic antagonists—including prazosin hydrochloride, terazosin hydrochloride, and its anhydrous form, terazosin hydrochloride anhydrous—are likewise employed to manage hyperplasia of the prostate. In contrast, testosterone propionate (testosteronpropionát) is contraindicated in patients with hyperplasia of the prostate, reflecting the need to avoid androgenic stimulation in this condition. Together, these relationships highlight the role of multiple α₁‑blockers in treating benign prostatic hyperplasia while emphasizing the avoidance of testosterone‑based therapy in affected individuals.

content: Alpha‑adrenoreceptor antagonists used to treat benign prostatic hypertrophy (BPH) include silodosin, terazosin, and the fixed‑dose combination of dutasteride with tamsulosin. Terazosin is classified within the broader group of endocrine drugs and produces both arterial and venous vasodilation, contributing to its therapeutic effects in BPH and related vascular conditions. The dutasteride/tamsulosin oral capsule contains dutasteride 0.5 mg and tamsulosin hydrochloride 0.4 mg, with tamsulosin hydrochloride serving as the active alpha‑adrenoreceptor‑blocking component. This combination exploits dutasteride’s 5‑alpha‑reductase inhibition together with tamsulosin’s alpha‑blockade to improve urinary flow in patients with BPH.
